data.title: DC crews pile snow at RFK Stadium site as city slowly digs out of storm
data.description:
Washington, D.C. Mayor Muriel Bowser said on Wednesday that the city has resorted to piling snow at Robert F. Kennedy Stadium amid growing frustration over the slow response to the storm.

“Ice and snow are not melting—so crews are hauling them to the RFK site,” Bowser wrote in a post on the social platform X, including a photo of a mound of snow.

“As of this morning, the mound was two stories high and the length of two football fields,” she continued.

Residents have struggled to walk on sidewalks, drive on roads or take scooters to their metro stops as the frigid temperatures have paralyzed normal operations.

Local public schools are reopening on Thursday but will operate on a two-hour delay for the rest of the week. By Wednesday evening, all major roadways were “passable,” all Metro stations were “clear and ready for operation,” and 53 of the 54 WMATA bus routes were up and running, Bowser said.

Bowser thanked residents for their patience Tuesday night, as officials continued to dig out side and residential roads, saying, “This winter storm was complex, and our response tactics have had to evolve. Ice, sleet, and snow—combined with persistent, below-freezing temperatures—have made clearing efforts particularly difficult.”

“DC, we appreciate your patience—our teams will be out until the work is done,” she continued.

In total, Reagan Washington National Airport, located outside of D.C. in Arlington, Va., recorded more than 6 inches of snow, according to local outlet Fox 5.

Dulles International Airport in Chantilly, Va., saw 7 inches, while Baltimore/Washington International Thurgood Marshall Airport faced more than 11 inches of snowfall, the news outlet said.

data.title: Duckworth fires back at Vance over Forrest Gump quip
data.description:
Sen. Tammy Duckworth (D-Ill.) fired back at Vice President Vance on Wednesday after he criticized her line of questioning at a Senate hearing, comparing her to the fictional character Forrest Gump, who is portrayed in the film as having a physical and intellectual disability.

Duckworth — a retired U.S. Army lieutenant colonel, who lost both her legs when her Black Hawk helicopter was shot down in Iraq — dismissed Vance’s criticism as “petty insults.”

“Forrest Gump ran toward danger in Vietnam. Your boss ran to his podiatrist crying bone spurs,” Duckworth wrote in a post on the social platform X, referring to President Trump’s medical draft deferments that excused him from serving in the Vietnam War.

“Petty insults at the expense of people with disabilities won’t change the fact that you’re risking troops’ lives to boost Chevron’s stock price,” she continued. “It’s my job to hold you accountable.”

Her comment was a direct response to Vance’s earlier post on X, which read, “Watching Tammy Duckworth obsessively interrupt Marco Rubio during this hearing is like watching Forest Gump argue with Isaac Newton.”

Illinois Gov. JB Pritzker (D) also came to Duckworth’s defense, responding to Vance’s post by saying, “That’s a U.S. Senator doing her job. This is a random troll tweeting at her.”

Her state’s official Democratic Party similarly backed Duckworth for asking tough questions.

“Illinois voters sent Tammy Duckworth to the U.S. Senate to ask tough questions and demand answers. Sneering at her for doing her job is vile. That kind of contempt says everything about JD Vance,” the Democratic Party of Illinois wrote in a post on X, responding to Vance.

The pointed jabs come after Duckworth engaged in a fiery back-and-forth with Secretary of State Marco Rubio over whether U.S. actions in the Caribbean are bound by the laws of war and whether U.S. troops risk being pulled into Venezuela.

“The president has already said that he was ready to put American troops in Venezuela,” Duckworth said.

Rubio responded: “No, I think the president said that he retains the right as commander in chief to protect the United States against any imminent threats. Maybe that involves troops, maybe that involves air power. We hope that, we don’t anticipate, that being the case in Venezuela.”

“I worry that you’re playing fast and loose with our nation,” Duckworth said, adding later, “Mr. Secretary, we don’t need to be in another forever war, and that is the path that we are going towards.”

data.title: Joaquin Castro shares Texas detention visit with 5-year-old boy, father captured in Minnesota
data.description:
Rep. Joaquin Castro (D-Texas) visited a 5-year-old and his father at the Dilley Immigration Processing Center in Dilley, Texas, where they are both being held following their arrests in Minnesota.

“I demanded his release and told him how much his family, his school, and our country loves him and is praying for him,” Castro wrote in a post on the social platform X, which was accompanied by a photo of a man holding the sleeping child in his arms.

On Monday, a federal judge temporarily blocked the removal of a 5-year-old boy and his father. The two were detained last week.

U.S. District Judge Fred Biery on Monday ruled that Department of Homeland Security officials cannot transfer 5-year-old Liam Conejo Ramos and his father, Adrian Conejo Arias, “outside of this judicial district during the pendency of this litigation and until further Order of this Court.”

The detainment of the 5-year-old has sparked outrage from families and lawmakers across the country. This was the fourth time a student in the Twin Cities school district was detained by federal officers during the Department of Homeland Security’s operations in the state, The Minnesota Star Tribune reported. The arrest was made after the boy came home from preschool, according to multiple media reports.

“This is a time where we should all be outraged,” Rep. Angie Craig (D-Minn) said in a video posted to X. “If this doesn’t pierce through your humanity, as a Republican member of Congress, if you can’t speak out about this, then you’ve got no humanity left.”

“I can’t even believe this is America. If you support this, you have lost all humanity,” she added in her caption.

However, Trump administration officials, including Border Patrol chief Gregory Bovino, defended the child’s detainment by stating U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement officers are “experts with children.”

“I will say unequivocally that we are experts in dealing with children,” Bovino said during a press conference last week. “Let me say that again, experts in dealing with children, not because we want to be, but because we have to be.”

data.title: Kid Rock calls for price cap on resale tickets to protect against gouging
data.description:
Kid Rock on Wednesday called for a price cap on resale tickets for live music and sporting events, arguing that fans and artists have been “getting screwed” by a system that drives up prices and offers little benefit to performers.  

“I’m here because hardworking Americans who love live music deserve better, and artists deserve control over their own work,” Kid Rock, whose real name is Robert Ritchie, told lawmakers during a Senate Commerce, Science and Transportation subcommittee hearing.

Kid Rock slammed the 2010 merger of Live Nation and Ticketmaster — which together control roughly 70 to 80 percent of the ticketing market for major U.S. concert venues — as a “monopoly dressed up as innovation,” saying it did not deliver on stated promises of increasing competition, lowering prices and helping performers. 

He suggested that the merger led the “economic foundation that supported artists in the past [to crumble],” pointing to concerns about piracy and secondary ticketing.

“Needless to say, that experiment has failed miserably. Independent venues have been crushed. Artists have lost leverage. Fans are paying more than ever and getting blamed for it,” Kid Rock said.

He called for artists to have more control over their ticket inventories and how they are sold, advocating for a 10 percent price cap in the resale market to curb price gouging and a ban on speculative pricing.

He also urged committee members to subpoena contracts and deals between artists, promoters, buildings, ticketing companies, agencies, and vendors, suggesting they would find “mountains of fraud and abuse.”  

Lawmakers have been pushing to regulate speculative pricing, a deceptive practice in which resellers list tickets that they do not yet own for sale on secondary platforms, for several years.

Subcommittee Chair Marsha Blackburn (R-Tenn.) described the schemes as “cheating consumers, circumventing safeguards, and undermining trust” in the live entertainment industry.

Congress passed the Better Online Ticket Sales, or BOTS, Act in 2016, which prohibits the use of automated software to bypass security measures or other controls to purchase tickets from online marketplaces.

The Department of Justice and dozens of state attorneys general sued Live Nation and Ticketmaster in May 2024 for alleged antitrust violations, two months after President Trump signed an executive order aimed at cracking down on ticket scalping and resale practices. Kid Rock was standing alongside Trump in the Oval Office when he signed that executive order.

A separate bill, the Transparency In Charges for Key Events Ticketing (TICKET) Act, which requires ticket sellers to disclose the total cost of tickets upfront to purchasers and regulates speculative ticketing, was approved by the Senate Commerce Committee last April and is awaiting action on the floor. It also ensures full refunds for canceled events.

Ticketmaster and Live Nation have been under congressional scrutiny for more than a decade, but their practices drew renewed attention following the 2022 ticketing debacle for Taylor Swift’s “Eras Tour.”

The Federal Trade Commission sued the two companies in September 2025, alleging they profited from fees while turning a blind eye to illegal resale tactics.

Dan Wall, the executive vice president of corporate and regulatory affairs for Live Nation, testified Wednesday that the company had made “big changes” in its policies to address the FTC’s concerns, particularly brokers using multiple accounts. He also defended the company’s practices, resisting the “monopoly” label.

 Brian Berry, the executive director of Ticket Policy Forum, and David Weingarden, the chair and co-founder of the Colorado Independent Venue Association, also testified on Wednesday, in addition to Kid Rock and Wall.

data.title: Walz: Trump overseeing Pretti shooting investigation 'so wrong on so many levels'
data.description:
Following the fatal shooting of Alex Pretti by federal officers in Minnesota last weekend, Gov. Tim Walz (D-Minn.) condemned Trump’s comments that he will personally oversee the investigation into the 37-year-old’s killing. 

President Trump told reporters at the White House on Tuesday that his administration will conduct a “big investigation” into the matter. 

“I want to see the investigation,” Trump said. “I’m going to be watching over it.”

In a street interview with NBC News on Wednesday, Walz called the president’s investigative oversight “wrong on so many levels.”

“It’s the last thing that we want,” the governor said. 

Pretti, an intensive care nurse at the Minneapolis VA hospital and Minnesota resident, was shot and killed by two federal agents on Saturday. He is the second person to be killed by federal immigration officers during the Trump administration’s massive immigration crackdown in the state. An Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officer fatally shot Renee Good, a mother of three, on Jan. 7. 

Walz’s comments follow a tumultuous week of conversations and public comments shared between federal leaders and Minnesota officials. 

Minnesota Bureau of Criminal Apprehension (BCA) Superintendent Drew Evans said during a press conference on Saturday that DHS had blocked the state agency’s access to the shooting scene, despite BCA obtaining a signed warrant. 

“We’re in uncharted territory here,” Evans said. “It’s been a long-standing understanding, both within our state and across the country, that entities like the BCA that conduct 80-plus percent of officer-involved shootings across the United States are asked to do these investigations of federal agents involved in officer-involved shootings.”

After a phone conversation with Trump on Monday, Walz said the president said he would look into pulling federal agents out of the state and said he would speak with DHS about ensuring Minnesota investigators’ access to Pretti case materials. 

Walz told NBC on Wednesday he has “no evidence” federal agents have begun leaving the state.

Additionally, both Walz and Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey (D) met with Trump’s Border Czar Tom Homan on Tuesday. Homan called the meeting “a productive starting point.”

In an interview with CNN’s Anderson Cooper on Tuesday evening, Walz called the meeting with the border czar “progress.”

“That’s progress, but they started this fire, so we’re not giving anybody credit for putting it out” the governor said. “But right now, what we need is we need a return to normalcy by the ICE agents out of here.”

data.title: Rubio says oil companies were not tipped off about Venezuela operation, contradicting Trump
data.description:
Secretary of State Marco Rubio said Wednesday that oil companies were not tipped off ahead of the Trump administration’s incursion into Venezuela, appearing to contradict past statements from President Trump.

“The oil executives were not involved whatsoever in any of the planning on this matter,” Rubio said during a Senate hearing. 

“All of those executives learned about this operation the same time everybody else did,” he added.

The comments come in contrast to those previously made by the president.

Asked soon after the Venezuela operation whether he spoke with oil companies before the capture of leader Nicolás Maduro, Trump said “yes.”

He told reporters he had spoken with oil executives “before and after” the military operation.

“And they want to go in, and they’re going to do a great job for the people of Venezuela, and they’re going to represent us well,” Trump said earlier this month.

data.title: Slovakia's PM rails against Politico report he was 'shocked' by Trump's psychological state
data.description:
Slovak Prime Minister Robert Fico fiercely denied a Politico report that he was “shocked” by President Trump’s state of mind during his visit to Mar-a-Lago this month.

Politico, citing five unnamed European diplomats, reported that Fico told EU leaders at a summit in Brussels last week that his meeting with the U.S. president left him concerned about Trump’s “psychological state.”

Two of the diplomats said Fico used the word “dangerous” to describe the way Trump came across during the Jan. 17 meeting at Trump’s estate in Florida, according to Politico.

“I must emphatically reject the lies of the POLITICO portal about how I assessed my meeting with US President D. Trump at an informal summit in Brussels,” Fico wrote in a post on the social platform X.

“No one heard anything, no one saw anything, there are no witnesses, but nothing prevented the POLITICO portal from coming up with lies,” he continued.

Attacking the “liberal” media, Fico wrote, “It is a sad look at the liberal and progressive political and media world. The abuse of criminal law to destroy opponents, the rejection of different opinions, boundless media lies, and attempts to assassinate political leaders in Slovakia and the US.”

The White House in a statement also dismissed the reporting.

“This is absolutely total fake news from anonymous European diplomats who are trying to be relevant. The meeting at Mar-a-Lago was positive and productive,” White House spokesperson Anna Kelly said.

Politico did not respond to a request for comment.

The news outlet reported that the remarks were made on the sidelines of the emergency EU summit, which was called to address the transatlantic alliance amid Trump’s threats to take control of Greenland and levy tariffs on countries that came to its defense. Trump has walked back his tariff threats.

Fico, who is among the most pro-Trump European leaders, denied speaking “at the informal summit in Brussels,” in his statement. He noted he was grateful the meeting with Trump was not canceled after he was critical of Trump’s approach in Venezuela.

“I agree with many strategies of the US president, but with some I don’t,” he wrote. “I honestly expected that after my sharp statement on Venezuela, my visit to the US would be cancelled. This did not happen, which makes me appreciate the meeting with the U.S. president all the more.”

data.title: Democratic senator calls on Trump to approve FEMA aid for Palisades fire recovery
data.description:
Sen. Richard Blumenthal (D-Conn.) on Wednesday called on President Trump to authorize $34 billion in Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) aid to help the California communities ravaged by the Palisades fire recover more than one year after the disaster struck.

Blumenthal, a ranking member of the Homeland Security and Government Affairs Permanent Subcommittee on Investigations, along with Chair Ron Johnson (R-Wis.) and members Sen. Rick Scott (R-Fla.) and Sen. Bernie Moreno (R-Ohio), held a hearing focused on details surrounding the fire.

“There have been delays … to local governments and authorities all across the country, including Florida, Louisiana, Connecticut and, yes, in California, despite numerous requests from California’s congressional delegation, the administration has yet to transmit to Congress the state’s request for $[34] billion in a supplemental appropriation for wildlife recovery,” Blumenthal said during the hearing.

He called on Johnson “to join me in asking the Trump administration to release that additional federal aid.” The chair later replied that the state’s FEMA request “is because of the utter incompetence and failure of Los Angeles and California elected officials,” and that the FEMA aid “should not be needed.”

“We’re almost $39 trillion in debt,” Johnson said moments later. “The federal government is bankrupt. Most of these funds are provided by local and state [governments], as is the hierarchy of emergency management. It starts with local and state. FEMA does finance after these disasters occur, some of them are unpreventable. Some, like the Palisades Fire, [are] entirely preventable.”

“Some American taxpayer is going to be, you know, ‘I’m supposed to join in a request for $[34] billion because California leadership utterly failed,'” the Wisconsin Republican continued. “And they were from running budget surpluses to huge budget deficits. I’m sorry, we have to start questioning this when we’re $39 trillion in debt.”

Republican members continued to point the blame at Los Angeles Mayor Karen Bass (D) and California Gov. Gavin Newsom (D), asking the witnesses about where the disconnect occurred between fire officials and city leaders. Moreno, for instance, asked where Ghana was in California, referring to Bass’s visit to the African country as part of part of a U.S. delegation attending the inauguration of Ghanaian President John Mahama.

“The mayor of Los Angeles is in a different continent, and then they all come back and come here to the federal government and say, ‘Oh, you know, we are complete idiots. Would you mind handing over $[34] billion?'” Moreno said.

Moreno criticized Blumenthal’s call for FEMA aid, asking why “would we reward that level of bad behavior?”

Blumenthal later pressed on the need for FEMA air to help “homeowners [and] small businesses recover.”

“Wisconsin suffered floods in August of last year, really disastrous floods,” he said. “And the president provided $29.8 million in September for recovery. Bravo. My point is FEMA, namely the current administrator, is delaying the funding that communities are due to recover those individuals who’ve been struck with disaster, but also to make them more resilient.”

“This idea that the secretary has to approve every contract worth $100,000 has delayed critical aid to those survivors,” Blumenthal said. “There’s no reason for it. There’s no precedent, and I would simply urge my colleagues to join me in demanding that FEMA do better.”

He later added that natural disasters “don’t really know the difference between red and blue states.”

Johnson replied back that FEMA funding is part of a six-bill funding package toward government departments, including the Department of Homeland Security (DHS), of which FEMA is a component. Democrats have threatened to block the passage of the DHS funding bill over Trump’s immigration crackdown after two U.S. citizens were killed during separate federal immigration enforcement operations this month, raising the possibility of a partial government shutdown.

“Talk about cutting your nose off to spite your face,” Moreno later said after reiterating Johnson’s response. “It’s cutting your nose, your ears and everything else. And they’re going to put American lives at risk.”

California lawmakers have called on the federal government to replenish FEMA’s Disaster Relief Fund and provide funding to other programs to help with recovery since last year. Former President Biden approved FEMA aid shortly after the fires erupted, himself at one point being stuck at Los Angeles International Airport due to 99 mph wind gusts, CNN reported at the time.

Wednesday’s hearing comes after Trump announced on Tuesday that he signed an executive order to speed up the rebuilding of homes in Pacific Palisades and Altadena, the California communities most affected by the fire. The order also directs FEMA and the Small Business Administration to issue regulations that preempt state and local regulations to expedite the building process.

Newsom, who was in Washington, D.C., Tuesday to advocate for the FEMA aid, called the executive order “useless.”

“Donald Trump has signed a useless Executive Order rather than deliver the $34 BILLION California’s tax payers DESERVE in wildfire disaster aid to actually rebuild!” he wrote on the social platform X. “It’s been over a year and he has refused to help the American people. Disgusting.”

Trump announced on Wednesday that Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) Administrator Lee Zeldin will oversee that his executive order is followed through.

“I haven’t told this to Lee Zeldin yet, but I’m going to put you in charge of getting permits,” Trump said at the Treasury Department’s Trump Accounts Summit. “I think we’re going to override to local authorities because they never gonna, they never gonna have it.”

“And when I was told that there’s a provision where you can override the local authority to get things done case of because it’s really to me, it’s a national emergency,” he added. “What they’ve done to these people is horrible.”

The Palisades and Eaton fires killed 31 people and destroyed around 13,000 residential properties after it burned for over three weeks. As of Jan. 7, one year after ashes from the Lachman Fire lit up what became the Palisades Fire, less than a dozen homes have been rebuilt in Los Angeles County with around 900 under construction.

The Associated Press contributed.

data.title: Mother planning a wedding, pilot killed in Maine plane crash
data.description:
Families and organizations have begun to identify the six victims killed in a Sunday plane crash in Maine.

Passengers were on board a Bombardier Challenger 600 that erupted in flames after an engine fire. 

Lakewood Church employee Shawna Collins died on the aircraft, a church employee told The Associated Press. She served at the mega church run by Joel Osteen Ministries.

“We can confirm with great sadness that a beloved member of our church staff and treasured friend, Shawna Collins, was on the plane that crashed in Maine on Sunday evening,” Lakewood Church said in an emailed statement, according to the Houston Chronicle. 

“She was a light that brightened our days, and she possessed a beautiful spirit that lifted everyone she met. We loved Shawna dearly, and we will miss her more than words can express. We pray for her entire family and offer them our love and support during this difficult time,” the church added.

The 53-year-old mother was helping to plan her daughter’s wedding at the time of her death.

“Everybody loved her. She just had that kind of personality,” church spokesperson Donald Iloff Jr. told the AP on Tuesday.

Collins was an event planner who organized international parties and celebrations for a wide range of clients, including Arnold & Itkin Trial Lawyers, whose co-founder is listed on the plane’s registration.

The firm has not confirmed if the co-founder was on board the deadly trip. 

Pilot Jacob Hosmer also died in the crash, his family confirmed Tuesday to the AP. The 47-year-old began working for Arnold & Itkin in 2025 as a “team captain,” per his LinkedIn.

The National Transportation Safety Board is still investigating the cause of the incident.

data.title: Erika Kirk pledges Turning Point will match $1,000 government deposits in 'Trump accounts'
data.description:
Turning Point USA CEO Erika Kirk said her organization will match the federal government’s $1,000 deposit in new “Trump accounts” for the children of its employees. 

Kirk wrote on the social platform X Wednesday that her husband, the late conservative activist and Turning Point founder Charlie Kirk, “spoke so often about the importance of young families and having children, and his face would light up every time he learned about a Turning Point employee welcoming a newborn into their family.”

“In that spirit, [Turning Point USA and Turning Point Action] are honored to continue Charlie’s support of families through a company-sponsored dollar-for-dollar match of the federal government’s $1,000 contribution to the [530A] ‘Trump Account’ established for every eligible employee’s newborn baby,” Kirk added.

“We’re proud to stand with @POTUS in supporting families and investing in the future of America,” she concluded.

The Treasury Department will contribute $1,000 to each account for children born between Jan. 1, 2025 and Dec. 31, 2028. Accounts are also available for children who do not turn 18 before the end of the calendar year in which their parents open an account, according to the IRS.

Multiple corporations, including BlackRock, Charles Schwab and SoFi, have also said they will match that $1,000 contribution to the children of their employees.

Parents can open a tax-free account for their child as of the start of tax season on Monday via elections on IRS Form 4547. The accounts were established by the One Big Beautiful Bill Act President Trump signed into law last July.

Beginning on July 5, people — including family members, friends and other adults — can contribute up to a combined $5,000 annually to an account, a limit that will increase with inflation starting in 2028. Employers of the child or their parent can contribute up to $2,500 per year, which counts against the $5,000 limit. 

Charitable organizations and state governments can also make contributions, which do not count against the annual limit. 

The funds in the account must be invested in certain mutual funds or exchange-traded funds that track the S&P 500 or “another index or primarily American equities,” the IRS says. The account can be accessed on Jan. 1 of the year in which the child turns 18.

At a Treasury summit earlier Wednesday, Trump said the accounts will help children “get a real start in life.”

“Decades from now, I believe the Trump accounts will be remembered as one of the most transformative policy innovations of all time,” the president predicted.
